title: The Invisible Foundation of Digital Sovereignty
content:
Sovereign Tech Agency CEO Adriana Groh was invited to contribute a guest article to Behörden Spiegel, a leading German publication on public administration, for its special issue on digital sovereignty. 

Below is the article in English; the German version is available here: 

„Digitale Souveränität in Aktion“

The publication can be requested free of charge and will be sent by email after submitting your contact details.

creator: info@sovereign.tech (Sovereign Tech Agency)

title: Global Infrastructure, Shared Responsibility — UN Open Source Week 2026
content:
For the fourth year in a row, we took part in UN Open Source Week in New York City. This year's participation made tangible progress toward better maintaining, protecting, and strengthening our digital common goods and global infrastructure through shared responsibility, collaboration, and international cooperation.

As the first European organization to strategically invest in open digital infrastructure, the Sovereign Tech Agency has a unique role at UN Open Source Week. We saw strong and consistent interest in our model, our approach to supporting the open source ecosystem, and the lessons we have learned from working directly with maintainers and communities.

Throughout the week, we focused on making our approach accessible so that other institutions can adapt and build upon it when considering their own initiatives. Across a variety of panels, workshops, and discussions, we shared not only what we do, but how we do it, and how we measure the impact of our initiatives.
snippet: For the fourth year in a row, we took part in UN Open Source Week in New York City. This year's participation made tangible progress toward better maintaining, protecting, and strengthening our
creator: info@sovereign.tech (Sovereign Tech Agency)

title: Maintain-a-thon 2.0 at UN Open Source Week 2026
content:
For the second year, the Sovereign Tech Agency and the United Nations Office for Digital and Emerging Technologies are convening open source maintainers in New York for a maintain-a-thon: bringing together the experts building and maintaining critical digital infrastructure.

The first maintain-a-thon showed the value of bringing open source maintainers together across projects, communities, and borders. While every project is different, participants discovered that many of the challenges they face in the open source ecosystem are shared. They learned from each other's approaches, shared practical solutions, and built new connections that continued beyond the event.
snippet: For the second year, the Sovereign Tech Agency and the United Nations Office for Digital and Emerging Technologies are convening open source maintainers in New York for a maintain-a-thon: bringing
creator: info@sovereign.tech (Sovereign Tech Agency)

title: Our maintainer delegation for UN Open Source Week
content:
Nine open source experts are joining the Sovereign Tech Agency at UN Open Source Week 2026 in New York. They maintain some of the world's most widely used open technologies, and they're bringing practitioner perspectives to an international forum on sustaining and securing open source infrastructure.

For the second year, the Sovereign Tech Agency is bringing a delegation of open source maintainers to UN Open Source Week in New York. The people who actually build and maintain critical digital infrastructure are rarely represented in the international conversations about how that infrastructure is sustained and maintained, or how to govern it. This expert delegation changes that, at our second annual maintain-a-thon, and across the full week of events, panels, and exchanges.
snippet: Nine open source experts are joining the Sovereign Tech Agency at UN Open Source Week 2026 in New York. They maintain some of the world's most widely used open technologies, and they're bringing
creator: info@sovereign.tech (Sovereign Tech Agency)

title: Newsletter: New partnership with DIN, insights from our survey, new fellows & hiring
content:
Email newsletter on 8 June 2026: New partnership with DIN, insights from our survey, new fellows & hiring

Last week, we announced a new partnership with DIN for the Sovereign Tech Standards network. Building on a newly signed memorandum of understanding, we will work together to strengthen open source participation in international standards development.

Before launching the Sovereign Tech Standards network, we wanted to hear from the maintainers whose open source work is affected by technical standards. We surveyed active open source contributors to understand how they view standards development and where the friction is. Now we’re sharing the survey findings that helped shape the pilot program.

The Sovereign Tech Fellowship is expanding. We are thrilled to introduce the 2026 cohort of maintainers, community managers, and technical writers.

Interested in joining the Sovereign Tech Agency? We’re currently hiring a Communications Lead for our team.

If you’d like to find out about these updates as they happen, you can find the Sovereign Tech Agency on Mastodon, LinkedIn, and Bluesky.
snippet: Email newsletter on 8 June 2026: New partnership with DIN, insights from our survey, new fellows & hiring
creator: info@sovereign.tech (Sovereign Tech Agency)

title: Meet the new Sovereign Tech Fellows
content:
After a successful pilot program in 2025, the Sovereign Tech Fellowship is expanding. The 2026 cohort brings together 14 maintainers, community managers, and technical writers working across Rust, Python, and a range of other critical open source ecosystems, with a shared focus on security, sustainability, and community

The Sovereign Tech Fellowship provides flexible, targeted support to the people who build and maintain critical open source infrastructure, enabling them to focus on the work that matters most. To ensure a lasting impact beyond the duration of the program, we are also fostering both peer-to-peer mentorship among the fellows and providing access to subject-matter experts.

The 2026 cohort includes three fellowship types:

  • Fellowships for maintainers support individuals directly working on critical open source infrastructure, typically through writing and reviewing code. All fellows in 2025 were in this category.
  • Fellowships for community managers, who are dedicated to growing and sustaining the communities that maintain critical infrastructure, for example by improving onboarding processes and engaging directly with new contributors.
snippet: After a successful pilot program in 2025, the Sovereign Tech Fellowship is expanding. The 2026 cohort brings together 14 maintainers, community managers, and technical writers working across Rust,
creator: info@sovereign.tech (Sovereign Tech Agency)

title: Where standards and code meet
content:
We have signed a memorandum of understanding with DIN, the German Institute for Standardization, to strengthen open source participation in international standards development.

Open source maintainers build digital infrastructure, but they’re rarely present where the technical rules governing these technologies are written. Participation in international standards bodies takes time, expertise, and sustained engagement that most independent maintainers simply cannot afford. This is the gap that the Sovereign Tech Standards network is designed to close. This year, we will support a pilot cohort of open source maintainers to participate in standards work at the IETF, W3C, and ISO, with compensation, onboarding, and mentoring to make sustained engagement possible.

Today we're announcing a new partnership that deepens that work.
snippet: We have signed a memorandum of understanding with DIN, the German Institute for Standardization, to strengthen open source participation in international standards development.
creator: info@sovereign.tech (Sovereign Tech Agency)

title: What open source maintainers told us about standards
content:
Earlier this year we ran a survey to understand how open source maintainers engage with standards work, and where the hurdles are. Here's what we learned, and how it shaped the new Sovereign Tech Standards network.

Open standards define how software interoperates, how data flows, and how secure our digital infrastructure is. The processes that produce them, however, are not equally open to everyone. We've written about why shaping open standards matters and how the new Sovereign Tech Standards network will bring open source experience into the standards development process. The IETF has long held that running code matters. A standard built with an early focus on real implementations is more likely to be clear, implementable, and interoperable than one developed in the abstract. Open source maintainers produce many of those implementations. When they are absent from or underrepresented in the processes that produce standards, specifications end up being tested against real-world conditions later than they should be.
snippet: Earlier this year we ran a survey to understand how open source maintainers engage with standards work, and where the hurdles are. Here's what we learned, and how it shaped the new Sovereign Tech
creator: info@sovereign.tech (Sovereign Tech Agency)

title: Join the Sovereign Tech Standards Network
content:
We’re launching the Sovereign Tech Standards network to support open source maintainers shaping standards and improving interoperability at IETF, W3C, and ISO. Apply by 19 May 2026.

Open standards are part of the invisible architecture of the digital world: they shape how systems communicate, how data flows, and how secure, and accessible digital infrastructure is. A technical standard isn’t software itself. Instead, it specifies how software should behave: the rules and requirements that ensure systems based on it function as intended and work together. When standards are open, practical to implement, and developed through transparent processes, they become a shared resource that keeps the digital commons secure and accessible to all. The Sovereign Tech Standards network will advance how open standards are created by involving the experts closest to the code.
snippet: We’re launching the Sovereign Tech Standards network to support open source maintainers shaping standards and improving interoperability at IETF, W3C, and ISO. Apply by 19 May 2026.
creator: info@sovereign.tech (Sovereign Tech Agency)

title: Documentation Sprint for Technical Writers
content:
As part of the Sovereign Tech Fellowship, we’re hosting a documentation sprint: up to six additional spots for technical writers who want to work on critical digital infrastructure in 2026. Apply by April 6, 2026!

Open digital infrastructure is not built by code alone. For projects to function and evolve over time, knowledge, processes, and decisions need to be clearly documented and accessible.

In many open source projects, this is a perennial challenge. Documentation is often incomplete, outdated, or difficult to navigate. New contributors struggle to find a clear starting point, workflows aren't defined, and important knowledge only exists in people’s minds. This creates friction in globally distributed communities and slows down the progress of FOSS projects.

Experienced technical writers contribute vital expertise: they give structure to complex content, make implicit knowledge easily accessible, and provide guidance for different audiences.
